coredata.dc:title: Surface ionization-induced tunable dynamic phosphorescence colors from carbon dots on paper for dynamic multimode encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qijun
Meng, Shuai
Li, Yuchen
Cheng, Dengke
Gu, Hailing
Zhao, Zhenxiao
Tang, Zikang
Tan, Jing
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: Stimuli-responsive phosphorescence materials with dynamic color-changing properties are highly promising for advanced information security. Herein, we demonstrate a novel strategy that effectively induces tunable dynamic phosphorescence color from carbon dots (CDs) on papers via surface ionization engineering for multimode encryption and information storage. Alkali-induced phenolic hydroxyl ionization on CDs can boost intersystem crossing rates, while surface-ionized groups with negative charges significantly passivate the surface defects and reduce non-radiative transitions, which can activate surface state-related blue phosphorescence emission. Combined with the carbon core state-related yellow-green phosphorescence, excitation/time-dependent phosphorescence color-changing from yellow-green to blue are realized from alkali (Na2CO3) treated CDs on paper. Furthermore, benefiting from the transition from NaHCO3 to Na2CO3 under thermal treatment, dynamic color-changing phosphorescence can also be activated by thermally stimulating NaHCO3-treated CDs on paper. Finally, multi-stimuli (alkali or thermal) and multi-modal dynamic phosphorescent colors from a single CDs system are realized for the first time. Based on these unique features, multimode encryption and smart 3D codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicine during transport or storage are demonstrated by combining an inkjet printing technique using CD inks.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, sb-CDs and sc-CDs inks

National Natural Science Foundation of China

Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152 , 62005106 and 61922091 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu ( BK20190864 and BK20190865 ), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture ( NY2021007 ), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR ( 0040/2019/A1 , 0128/2020/A3 , 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ ), and Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME, MYRG2020-00164-IAPME). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS analysis.

The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152, 62005106 and 61922091), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (BK20190864 and BK20190865), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture (NY2021007), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0128/2020/A3, 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ), and Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME, MYRG2020-00164-IAPME). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for CD@paper, sb-CD@paper and sc-CD@paper, and their phosphorescence emission properties. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–c) TEM images of the CDs, sb-CDs and sc-CDs, and the inset is the corresponding HRTEM images. (d) FTIR spectra of CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs. (e) EDS spectra of CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–visible absorption of CDs, sb-CDs and sc-CDs. (b) Phosphorescence emission spectra of the CD@paper, sb-CD@paper and sc-CD@paper after 365 nm light irradiation. (c) Phosphorescence emission spectra of the sc-CD@paper after irradiation with lights of different wavelengths. (d) Time-resolved phosphorescence emission spectra of sc-CD@paper after excitation at 365 nm. (e) CIE 1931 coordinates of time-dependent color of sc-CD@paper. (f) Phosphorescence decay curves of sc-CD@paper monitored at 480 nm and 550 nm after 365 nm light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Illustration of information encryption and processes using CDs via adding alkali and heating. (b) Schematic illustration of information encryption and decryption processes using aqueous CDs, sc-CDs and sb-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Smart 3D codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicine during transport or storage. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Possible photoluminescence emission processes and energy level diagram ofCD@paper, sb-CD@paper and sc-CD@paper. (Fluor., fluorescence; Tsur.: surface triplet state; Tcore.: inner core triplet state).

head.ce:title: Surface ionization-induced tunable dynamic phosphorescence colors from carbon dots on paper for dynamic multimode encryption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Micro-nano Optoelectronics and Terahertz Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimuli-responsive phosphorescence materials with dynamic color-changing properties are highly promising for advanced information security. Herein, we demonstrate a novel strategy that effectively induces tunable dynamic phosphorescence color from carbon dots (CDs) on papers via surface ionization engineering for multimode encryption and information storage. Alkali-induced phenolic hydroxyl ionization on CDs can boost intersystem crossing rates, while surface-ionized groups with negative charges significantly passivate the surface defects and reduce non-radiative transitions, which can activate surface state-related blue phosphorescence emission. Combined with the carbon core state-related yellow-green phosphorescence, excitation/time-dependent phosphorescence color-changing from yellow-green to blue are realized from alkali (Na2CO3) treated CDs on paper. Furthermore, benefiting from the transition from NaHCO3 to Na2CO3 under thermal treatment, dynamic color-changing phosphorescence can also be activated by thermally stimulating NaHCO3-treated CDs on paper. Finally, multi-stimuli (alkali or thermal) and multi-modal dynamic phosphorescent colors from a single CDs system are realized for the first time. Based on these unique features, multimode encryption and smart 3D codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicine during transport or storage are demonstrated by combining an inkjet printing technique using CD inks.

Multistimuli and excitation-dependent dynamic phosphorescence colors from a single CD system were realized for the first time for dynamic multimode encryption. Dynamic phosphorescence colors can be modulated from the dual phosphorescent centers of CDs in the surface triplet state and the carbon core-related triplet state, where the surface triplet state can be activated by external stimuli (alkali or thermal).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technologies play an integral role in securing information and combating the increasing sophistication of counterfeiting [1]. Phosphorescent materials demonstrate huge potential in these advanced technologies owing to their long-lasting persistent luminescence and visual identifiability [2–13]. However, most phosphorescent materials show static luminescent outputs (untunable color), resulting in low-level readout and decoding for data encryption. Stimuli-responsive phosphorescence materials with dynamic color-changing properties can provide multiple channels within spatially limited platforms, thus promoting more reliable encryption at much higher levels [14,15]. However, realizing such properties in a single material remains a formidable challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a newly emerged phosphorescent nanomaterial, have attracted considerable attention because of their facile preparation, low cost, high photostability, and low toxicity [16–21]. CDs with tunable chemical structures can be designed to form triplet energy level structures with considerable coupling efficiencies to achieve phosphorescence. To date, several rational strategies [22–31] have been proposed to enhance the phosphorescence of CDs, and phosphorescence color-tunability or stimuli-responsive emissions from CD-based materials [23,32–36] have been reported. Yang et al. prepared a series of multicolor phosphorescence CDs (green, yellow-green, and yellow) by changing the degree of carbonization [32]. Our group recently reported the first example of CDs with time-dependent phosphorescence colors (TDPCs) [33]. Lin et al. demonstrated the first example of ﬂuorescence-to-phosphorescence conversion of CDs via an external heating stimulus [34]. Our group demonstrated an example of phosphorescent responsiveness of CDs/PVA with water and heat stimulation [35]. However, the development of multistimuli-responsive and multimode tunable dynamic color-changing phosphorescence in a single CD system remains a formidable challenge. To the best of our knowledge, stimuli-responsive phosphorescent CD materials with multimode dynamic color-changing properties have not yet been reported.

Considering the unique carbon core state and surface state luminescence mechanism of CDs, the realization of tunable dynamic phosphorescence color is possible by constructing multiple phosphorescence centers in CDs. Notably, surface state phosphorescence is easily affected by the external environment, while the core state is relatively stable. Oxygen doping into CDs can promote intersystem crossing (ISC) rates, which facilitate the formation of surface triplet state centers. Typically, aromatic carbonyl groups can serve as surface triplet state centers to produce phosphorescence owing to the existence of the n-π∗ electronic configuration [24,37,38]. However, carbonyl groups are very stable and do not easily respond to external stimuli. Compared with aromatic carbonyl groups, hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of CDs are more susceptible to external stimuli to produce changes in the electron absorption configuration, which may induce the formation of a new surface triplet center. In addition, our previous study showed that carbon core state phosphorescence can be inherited from N-containing heterocyclic precursors [25,35]. We reasoned that the surface triplet state of CDs with abundant surface OH groups and N-doped inner cores can be activated by proper external stimuli, and by combining with the carbon core-related triplet state, multistimuli-responsive and tunable dynamic phosphorescence colors can be realized from a single CD system.

To validate our hypothesis, CDs with N-doped carbon core and surface phenolic hydroxyl groups were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal treatment of folic acid. Owing to the presence of the N-doped carbon core, the CDs on the papers exhibited dominant yellow-green phosphorescence with less excitation-dependent colors. After surface ionization treatment with sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), alkali-induced phenolic hydroxyl ionization boosted the ISC rates of the surface state, while the surface-ionized groups with negative charges greatly passivated the surface defects and reduced non-radiative transitions, resulting in surface state-related blue phosphorescence emission. Combined with the carbon core state-related yellow-green phosphorescence, the phosphorescence colors of CDs on papers could be tuned by excitations, and time, realizing dynamic color tuning from yellow-green to blue in multimode. Furthermore, our results showed that the weak base sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) could not easily ionize the phenolic hydroxyl groups to produce dynamic phosphorescence color-changing. Benefiting from the transition from NaHCO3 to Na2CO3 under thermal treatment, dynamic color-changing phosphorescence can also be activated by the thermal treatment of NaHCO3-treated CDs on paper. Finally, multistimuli (alkali or thermal) and excitation-dependent dynamic phosphorescence colors from a single CD system were realized. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first case of realizing multifunctional properties based on a single CD system. Based on these unique features, multiple dimensions, including excitation/time-dependent, alkali and heat stimuli-responsive, anti-counterfeiting techniques, and smart three-dimensional (3D) codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicine during transport or storage were developed by combining an inkjet printing technique using the prepared CD inks. To verify the applicability of 3D codes, a smart app was designed to decode the dynamic color transformation of 3D codes, enabling the authentication and real-time monitoring of medicine.

Unless otherwise noted, all reagents were used without purification. Folic acid, sodium bicarbonate, sodium carbonate and sodium borohydride were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals. Deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ cm at 25 °C) obtained using a Milli-Q (MQ) water system was used in all the experiments.

Pristine CDs were synthesized similar to our previous method [25]. Briefly, folic acid (0.3 g), deionized water (40 ml) and HCL (0.5 ml) added to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 250 °C for 3 h. After completion of the hydrothermal carbonization process, a dark brown dispersion was obtained, which was dialyzed with deionized water using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 12 h, and then freeze-dried to obtain the powder sample for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, sb-CDs and sc-CDs inks
ce:section.ce:para: Two equal amounts of CDs aqueous solutions (5 mg/ml) were added with 0.05 g/ml sodium bicarbonate and 0.05 g/ml sodium carbonate, and stirred for 5 min, and finally sb-CDs and sc-CDs aqueous solutions were prepared, respectively. The CDs, sb-CDs and sc-CDs solutions were directly used as inks by injecting into a vacant cartridge of a commercial ink-jet printer (EPSON L3150 printer).

Hydrothermally synthesized CDs were prepared as described in our previous study [25]. In brief, folic acid (0.3 g), deionized water (40 mL), and HCl (0.5 mL) were heated at 250 °C for 3 h in an autoclave and then cooled to room temperature (Fig. 1 ). The obtained dark brown solution was dialyzed with deionized water through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for 12 h and then freeze-dried to give the dark product of CDs. To illustrate the contributions of the different oxygen-related group ionization on phosphorescence, their surface oxygen-related groups were selectively ionized using sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), and the resulting samples were denoted as sb-CDs and sc-CDs, respectively. After printing on paper, the resulting samples were denoted as sb-CD@paper and sc-CD@paper, respectively. A yellow-green phosphorescence can be observed in CD@paper and sb-CD@paper after ceasing UV irradiation. Remarkably, the intensity and lifetimes of RTP for sb-CD@paper were significantly higher than those of CD@paper after UV excitation. The sc-CD@paper is capable of exhibiting multimode color tuning of phosphorescence from yellow-green to blue after the cessation of light irradiation depending on the time and excitation changing modes. These unique phenomena inspired us to examine their photophysical properties in depth.

The morphologies of the pristine CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs were analysed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 2 a–c). The three types of CDs exhibited uniform and well-dispersed quasi-spherical morphologies with diameters ranging from 2 to 5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show identical lattice fringes of 0.21 nm in size, which correspond to graphene (100) planes,[39]indicating a similar core structure. These results indicate that the surface functionalization of CDs did not change their morphology.

The CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs were investigated using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Fig. 2d). The FTIR spectrum of the CDs has a broad peak with a maximum at ∼3400 cm−1 corresponding to the O–H stretching vibrations and several sharper peaks corresponding to CO stretching of undissociated carboxylic acid at 1716 cm−1, and C–OH stretching at 1220 cm−1 [40–43], indicating that the CDs were covered with hydroxyl and.

Carboxyl functional groups. The absorption band at 1300–1500 cm−1 is ascribed to the nitrogen heterocycle [33,42], which is inherited from the folic acid precursor. Compared with that of CDs, the absorption band of sb-CDs and sc-CDs at 1716 cm−1 due to CO stretching of undissociated carboxylic acid disappeared and gave rise to a new peak at 1590 cm−1 for carboxylates, suggesting the deprotonation of carboxyl groups for sb-CD and sc-CDs. Furthermore, for sc-CDs, it is clearly seen that the C–OH stretching of the phenolic group at 1250 cm−1 disappears and a new peak is observed at 1300 cm−1, corresponding phenolate group stretching. Thus, these results reveal that the carboxylic groups on the CD surface can be ionized by NaHCO3 and Na2CO3, while the hydroxyl groups can only be ionized by Na2CO3. The chemical compositions of the CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs were also investigated by EDS (Fig. 2e and Table S1). The EDS spectrum of the CDs shows four peaks at 0.27, 0.39, 0.53, and 1.05 keV, corresponding to C, N, O, and Na, respectively. Compared with sb-CDs, the higher Na content of sc-CDs implies the ionization of hydroxyl groups in sc-CDs, which causes more Na ions to bind to the sc-CDs surface. In addition, zeta potential measurements were performed on the CD, sb-CD, and sc-CD samples. The zeta potentials of CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs in aqueous solution were −7.6, −11 and −19.4 mV, respectively. The more negative surface potential of sc-CDs in aqueous solution could be attributed to the deprotonation of hydroxyl, which produced more negatively ionized radicals. It is known that the deprotonation of phenolic hydroxyl groups usually requires a higher pH than that of carboxyl groups, which is consistent with the above results.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of CD, sc-CD, and sb-CD aqueous solutions were further studied in Fig. 3 a. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CDs displays a broad band peaked at 320 nm, which is attributed to the aromatic sp2 domains in the carbon core [40,44]. Compared with CDs, the sb-CDs exhibit an absorption red shift, which may be attributed to the electron conjugation caused by the ionization of the carboxyl group attached to the carbogenic core. A pronounced absorption red shift was detected for sc-CDs compared to sb-CDs, indicating the deprotonation of the phenolic hydroxyl groups, which causes further electron conjugation. Notably, for sc-CDs, an obvious broad absorption band appears at longer wavelengths, which possibly arises because of the enhanced n-π∗ transition caused by the deprotonation of phenolic hydroxyl groups [44]. The phenolic hydroxyl group contains two lone pairs of electrons. When the phenate anion is formed, the lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom increases to three pairs, which greatly strengthens the n-π∗ conjugation effect, resulting in a red-shifted and enhanced absorption.

In the phosphorescence spectra, both CD@paper and sb-CD@paper exhibited dominant yellow-green phosphorescence with less excitation-dependent color (Fig. S1). However, the intensity and average lifetimes of phosphorescence for sb-CD@paper significantly increased compared to that of CD@paper after excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 3b and Fig. S2 and Table S2). The time-resolved phosphorescence emission spectra show no obvious color change.

With time after irradiation at 335, 365, and 395 nm (Fig. S3), which is in good agreement with the naked eye observation. The yellow-green phosphorescence results from the N-doped carbon core triplet state were similar to what was observed in our previous reports [25]. It is worth mentioning that folic acid with a nitrogen heterocyclic structure can exhibit yellow-green phosphorescence after printing them into the paper (Fig. S4a). The nitrogen heterocyclic structure of folic acid was inherited by the CDs, as shown in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S4b), which further confirms the yellow-green phosphorescence derived from the N-doped carbon core state. Compared with folic acid on paper, CD@paper exhibits a small red-shift region emission, which is mainly attributed to the conjugation expansion of nitrogen heterocycles during CDs formation progress [25,35].

In contrast to the CD@paper and sb-CD@paper, sc-CD@paper displays obvious excitation-dependent phosphorescence colors in the yellow-green and blue regions after different irradiations (Fig. 3c), which implies the presence of dual phosphorescence centers in the CDs [35]. Compared to the CD@paper, sc-CD@paper exhibits a small red-shift region emission, which is mainly attributed to the electron conjugation expansion caused by deprotonation of the phenolic hydroxyl groups. Notably, sc-CD@paper displays an unpredictable TDPC emission, and such a TDPC phenomenon could only be detected after 365 nm light irradiation (Fig. 3d). After irradiation with wavelengths longer or shorter than 365 nm (e.g. 335 and 395 nm, respectively), sc-CD@paper only emitted blue or yellow-green phosphorescence with time, respectively (Figs. S5a and b). As shown in Fig. 3d, the time-resolved phosphorescence spectra exhibited a yellow-green phosphorescence with a maximum at 550 nm at 0.01 s. The yellow-green phosphorescence (at 550 nm) decays faster than the blue phosphorescence (at 480 nm), leading to a dynamic phosphorescence color change from yellow-green to blue. The CIE diagrams clearly show that the color evolution varied with excitation (Fig. 3e), which agrees well with the naked eye observation. The emission lifetime at 550 nm was calculated to be 729 ms after excitation with 365 nm light, which is much longer than the 521 ms of sb-CD@paper. In addition, the blue phosphorescence emission lifetime at 480 nm was as long as 949 ms (Fig. 3f, Table S2). Under 365 nm light excitation, sc-CD@paper exhibited a phosphorescence quantum yield of 2.6%, much higher than 0.8% of CD@paper. Evidently, the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl on the CD surface not only greatly enhances the yellow-green phosphorescence emission, but also induces efficient blue phosphorescence, so as to finally realize dynamic phosphorescence color change depending on time and excitation changing modes.

It is well known that NaHCO3 is very unstable, and it decomposes into Na2CO3 under thermal stimulation. After the sb-CDs solution was heated at 50 °C for 1 h, the pH of the solution increased from 9.5 to 12.5, which indicated the transition from weak base NaHCO3 to strong base Na2CO3. Benefiting from the transition from NaHCO3 to Na2CO3, dynamic color-changing phosphorescence can also be activated by under thermal stimulus (Fig. S5c).

Based on the above characterizations and analyses, a possible mechanism for the surface ionization engineering-induced color-tunable phosphorescence of CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The prepared CDs are composed of a N-doped carbon core and hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface, of which the N-doped carbon core is suggested to account for the observed yellow-green phosphorescence. In the presence of a strong alkali (Na2CO3), the phenolic group on the CD surface undergoes ionization, and this ionization-induced delocalization effect greatly promotes the n-π∗ transition by increasing the number of lone-pair electrons, thus decreasing singlet-triplet splitting. As a result, ISC rates are greatly promoted, triggering the formation of a surface triplet state with higher energy. In addition, non-radiative decay from the excited state is greatly suppressed by decreased surface defects by surface-ionized groups, and could therefore lead to the observed bright blue phosphorescence after higher energy light irradiation. An N-doped carbon core state with a lower energy can emit yellow-green phosphorescence. Under suitable energy light irradiation (around 365 nm), two phosphorescent centers can be activated simultaneously and generate a faster decayed yellow-green phosphorescence and a slower decayed blue phosphorescence, thus leading to the observed TDPCs phenomenon. After irradiation with wavelengths longer than 365 nm, the sc-CD@paper only shows yellow-green phosphorescence color, which is because only N-doped carbon core state centers can be efficiently activated under low-energy light irradiation. However, for sb-CD@paper, the weak alkalinity of NaHCO3 is not strong enough to ionize the phenolic hydroxyl group. Therefore, sb-CD@paper exhibits a single yellow-green phosphorescent emission similar to that of CD@paper. However, the ionization of oxygen-containing groups can reduce non-radiative transitions, thus leading to the observed phosphorescence enhancement phenomenon compared to CD@paper. Benefiting from the transition from NaHCO3 to Na2CO3 under thermal treatment, dynamic color-changing phosphorescence can also be activated by the thermal treatment of NaHCO3-treated CDs on paper.

To further prove that the ionization of phenolic hydroxyl groups could facilitate surface state blue phosphorescence emission, CDs with more phenolic hydroxyl groups were prepared by treating them with sodium borohydride (named rCDs). After ionization with Na2CO3, the phosphorescence was measured by printing CDs on paper (sc-rCD@paper). The infrared spectra of the rCD results indicate that sodium borohydride could reduce the CO to O–H bonds, thereby generating more hydroxyl groups on the surface of the rCDs (Fig. S6a). Compared with sc-CD@paper, sc-rCD@paper possesses higher intensity and longer lifetime in the phosphorescence after excitation at 335 nm (Figs. S6b and c and Tables S2 and 3), indicating the generation of more surface state triplet excitons. In addition, compared with the time-dependent colors of sc-CD@paper, sc-rCD@paper exhibits invariable blue phosphorescence after excitation at 365 nm (Fig. S6d), suggesting that blue phosphorescence can be further enhanced by ionization of more phenolic hydroxyl groups.

Furthermore, the photostability of sc-CD@paper was evaluated by repeated excitation with 365 nm light for 100 times. The results showed that sc-CD@paper did not undergo significant photobleaching (Fig. S7a). The prepared sc-CD@paper exhibited the phosphorescence even when placed in an ambient environment for more than 30 days (Fig. S7b), which indicates its good stability.

Based on the unusual phosphorescence properties of CDs, we further demonstrated the potential of these CDs for advanced information encryption and decryption by combining an inkjet printing technique. As shown in Figs. S7 and 2D codes (Fig. S8a) and landscape painting (Fig. S8b) were printed on filter paper using the sc-CD ink, which exhibited multicolor phosphorescence (blue and yellow-green) after irradiation with 335 and 365 nm light, respectively. Impressively, only dynamic phosphorescence color transformation could be observed by the naked eye after the 365 nm lamp was removed. A type of multiple dimensional, including excitation/time-dependent, alkali, and heat stimuli-responsive anti-counterfeiting techniques were presented in Fig. 4 a. A flower pattern was printed on a filter paper using the CD ink, and it exhibited dominant yellow-green phosphorescence after irradiation with 335 and 365 nm light (Fig. 4a). When the petals and stem parts were stimulated by NaHCO3 and Na2CO3 inks respectively, the symbol petals showed excitation/time-dependent phosphorescence colors, while the stem parts still showed yellow-green phosphorescence. Interestingly, after applying thermal stimulation to the designated zones of flowers, dynamic phosphorescence color transformation could be observed from the flower stem after removing the 365 nm lamp or by changing the excitation wavelength because of the thermally induced conversion of NaHCO3 to Na2CO3. In addition, advanced multilevel anti-counterfeiting and information encryption were constructed, as shown in Fig. 4b. The symbol “888” was painted on filter paper using CDs, sb-CDs, and sc-CDs inks. Normally, encrypted information cannot be recognized under daylight or UV light. After removing the 365 nm lamp after 3 s, the first-level encrypted code “520” can be reorganized owing to the longer phosphorescence lifetime of the sc-CDs compared to that of the sb-CDs and CDs. When the heating treatment is implemented, the encrypted information “928” at a deeper level can be clearly read. Importantly, the decrypted code can be re-encrypted via HCL wetting.

Given the time-dependent and thermal stimuli-responsive phosphorescence, we further envision the potential application of these dynamic phosphorescence CDs as smart 3D codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicines during transportation or storage. Many medicines or foods should typically be stored below room temperature (e.g. 30 °C) to retain their shelf life. However, due to improper operation, medicines may be destroyed at high temperatures during transportation or storage, which greatly shortens their shelf lives. These problems cannot be visually detected, possibly leading to a potential public hygiene hazard. Here, we designed a new type of 3D code based on dynamic color transformation CDs that carry multiple types of information for anti-counterfeiting and monitoring of medicines. To verify the applicability of 3D codes, a smart app was designed to decode the dynamic color transformation 3D codes. As shown in Fig. 5 and Movie S1, under UV 365 nm excitation on or off, the dynamic 3D codes carrying different aspects of the medicine are displayed. First, the fluorescence pattern is reorganized with the first-level information linked to the morphology of medicine. After turning off the UV lamp, the second-level information B representing the mark of qualified products appeared at 0.1 s, indicating that the product is genuine. If the medicine is maintained below room temperature during transportation and storage, the shelf life information of the medicine decrypted by the app will be displayed. However, if the medicine is stored above 50 °C for more than 1 h at once, a warning message, Info D, could be read from 3D code, which indicates potential deterioration of the medicine.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.03.063.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

In summary, we developed a new strategy using phenolic hydroxyl ionization to induce CD surface state phosphorescence to achieve external stimulus-tunable dynamic phosphorescence color outputs. It was revealed that phenolic hydroxyl ionization has great utility in enhancing ISC rates, passivating surface defects and reducing non-radiative decay, thereby inducing surface state-relative blue phosphorescence. Combined with the carbon core state-related yellow-green phosphorescence, the phosphorescence colors of CDs could be tuned by time evolution, excitation, alkali, and heat, realizing multimode dynamic color tuning from yellow-green to blue. Owing to their unique multimode dynamic phosphorescence color properties, multimode encryption and smart 3D codes with anti-counterfeiting and monitoring functions for medicine during transport or storage have been developed. Our work provides a facile strategy for realizing multi-stimuli and multi-modal dynamic phosphorescent colors in a single CD system, which facilities development of multicolor, multitemporal and multimodal phosphorescence materials. Moreover, we expect that this study may offer exciting opportunities for designing advanced information encryption.

Qijun Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shuai Meng: Resources, Visualization. Yuchen Li: Resources, Visualization. Dengke Cheng: Resources, Visualization. Hailing Gu: Resources, Visualization. Zikang Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Jing Tan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Songnan Qu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52002152, 62005106 and 61922091), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu (BK20190864 and BK20190865), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Zhenjiang-Modern Agriculture (NY2021007), the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1, 0128/2020/A3, 0131.2020.A3 and 0073/2019/AMJ), and Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME, MYRG2020-00164-IAPME). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Wang Weiwei from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.03.063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical nanomaterials and enabling technologies for high-security-level anticounterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ren, G. Lin, C. Clarke, J. Zhou, D. Jin, Optical nanomaterials and enabling technologies for high-security-level anticounterfeiting, Adv. Mater., 32, 2019, 1901430.
W. Zhao, Z. He, B.Z. Tang, Room-temperature phosphorescence from organic aggregates, Nat. Rev. Mater., 5, 2020, 869-885.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activating efficient phosphorescence from purely organic materials by crystal design

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Bolton, K. Lee, H.J. Kim, K.Y. Lin, J. Kim, Activating efficient phosphorescence from purely organic materials by crystal design, Nat. Chem., 3, 2011, 205-210.
